annotations.label: Fubini's theorem (informal)

anchor.snippet: Fubini's theorem gives the conditions under which a double integral can be computed as an iterated integral

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Fubini for integrable functions on a product of σ-finite spaces as `MeasureTheory.integral_prod`.

annotations.label: Fubini's theorem (Lebesgue integrable)

anchor.snippet: More formally, the theorem states that if a function is Lebesgue integrable on a rectangle

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.integral_prod` states ∫ f d(μ.prod ν) = ∫∫ f x y dν dμ for integrable f.

annotations.label: Tonelli's theorem (introductory)

anchor.snippet: Tonelli's theorem , introduced by Leonida Tonelli in 1909, is similar but is applied to a non-negative measurable function

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.lintegral_prod` is Tonelli's theorem for non-negative measurable functions (ℝ≥0∞-valued lintegral).

annotations.label: Fubini–Tonelli theorem (introductory)

anchor.snippet: The Fubini and Tonelli theorems are usually combined and form the Fubini–Tonelli theorem

annotations.note: The combined Fubini–Tonelli is captured by `MeasureTheory.integral_prod` together with `MeasureTheory.lintegral_prod`.

annotations.label: Fubini's theorem for infinite series (Pringsheim)

anchor.snippet: A related result is often called Fubini's theorem for infinite series

annotations.note: `Summable.tsum_prod` (and `tsum_comm`) gives the Fubini-style interchange of double summations for summable families.

annotations.label: Product measure

anchor.snippet: A measure μ on X × Y is called a product measure

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Measure.prod` defines the product measure on X × Y satisfying `prod_prod : μ.prod ν (s ×ˢ t) = μ s * ν t`.

annotations.label: Maximal product measure

anchor.snippet: There is always a unique maximal product measure on X × Y

annotations.note: Mathlib defines a canonical product measure (uniquely determined under σ-finiteness) but does not develop the general theory of distinct/maximal product measures.

annotations.label: Fubini's theorem for integrable functions

anchor.snippet: Fubini's theorem states that if f is X × Y integrable

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.integral_prod` is exactly Fubini for product-integrable functions on σ-finite spaces.

annotations.label: Tonelli's theorem

anchor.section: Tonelli's theorem for non-negative measurable functions
anchor.snippet: Tonelli's theorem states that if

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.lintegral_prod` is Tonelli's theorem for AEMeasurable ℝ≥0∞-valued functions on σ-finite spaces.

annotations.label: Interchange of summations (Tonelli special case)

anchor.section: Tonelli's theorem for non-negative measurable functions
anchor.snippet: A special case of Tonelli's theorem is in the interchange of the summations

annotations.note: Interchange of non-negative double sums is available as `ENNReal.tsum_comm` and `Summable.tsum_prod`/`tsum_comm`.

annotations.label: Fubini–Tonelli theorem

anchor.snippet: Combining Fubini's theorem with Tonelli's theorem gives the Fubini–Tonelli theorem

annotations.note: The combined Fubini–Tonelli statement follows from `integral_prod` paired with `lintegral_prod`/`hasFiniteIntegral_prod_iff` in `Mathlib.MeasureTheory.Integral.Prod`.

annotations.label: Fubini's theorem for complete measures

anchor.snippet: For this reason, one sometimes uses versions of Fubini's theorem for complete measures

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Fubini for the standard (non-completed) product measure and has the null-measurable / completion machinery in `Mathlib.MeasureTheory.Measure.NullMeasurable`, but no separately stated Fubini specifically for the completion.

annotations.label: Failure of Tonelli's theorem for non σ-finite spaces

anchor.section: Failure of Tonelli's theorem for non σ-finite spaces
anchor.snippet: Suppose that X is the unit interval with the Lebesgue measurable sets and Lebesgue measure

annotations.note: No counterexample of this form (non σ-finite failure of Tonelli) is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Failure of Fubini's theorem for non-maximal product measures

anchor.section: Failure of Fubini's theorem for non-maximal product measures
anchor.snippet: if we give X × Y the product measure such that the measure of a set is the sum of the Lebesgue measures of its horizontal sections

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize counterexamples concerning non-maximal product measures.

annotations.label: Failure of Tonelli's theorem for non-measurable functions

anchor.section: Failure of Tonelli's theorem for non-measurable functions
anchor.snippet: Suppose that X is the first uncountable ordinal

annotations.note: No Mathlib counterexample using the first uncountable ordinal for Tonelli failure was found.

annotations.label: Failure of Fubini's theorem for non-measurable functions

anchor.section: Failure of Fubini's theorem for non-measurable functions
anchor.snippet: A variation of the example above shows that Fubini's theorem can fail for non-measurable functions

annotations.note: No corresponding non-measurable Fubini counterexample is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sierpiński's continuum-hypothesis example

anchor.section: Failure of Fubini's theorem for non-measurable functions
anchor.snippet: Wacław Sierpiński demonstrated that assuming the continuum hypothesis

annotations.note: Mathlib has no CH-based Sierpiński counterexample to Fubini.

annotations.label: Failure of Fubini's theorem for non-integrable functions (sign example)

anchor.section: Failure of Fubini's theorem for non-integrable functions
anchor.snippet: A simple example to show that the repeated integrals can be different in general is to take the two measure spaces to be the positive integers

annotations.note: No formalized counterexample showing failure of iterated-sum equality on ℕ × ℕ was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Product of two integrals formula

anchor.snippet: For the product of two integrals with lower limit zero and a common upper limit we have the following formula

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.integral_prod_mul` gives ∫∫ f x · g y = (∫ f)(∫ g), the general form of the product-of-integrals identity.

annotations.label: Arcsine integral via Fubini

anchor.snippet: Values of the inverse sine integral can be determined by exchanging the order of integration using Fubini's theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Real.arcsin` but does not formalize this Fubini-based evaluation of the inverse-sine integral.

annotations.label: Dirichlet eta function

anchor.snippet: The Dirichlet series defines the Dirichlet eta function as follows

annotations.note: No `dirichletEta`/`riemannEta` definition is present in Mathlib (only `riemannZeta` and related L-functions).

annotations.label: Dirichlet eta value via Fubini

anchor.snippet: can be proven using Fubini's theorem

annotations.note: Since the Dirichlet eta function itself is not in Mathlib, this Fubini-based evaluation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Integral of complete elliptic integral of the first kind

anchor.snippet: The improper integral of the complete elliptic integral of the first kind

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the complete elliptic integral K, so this identity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Integral of complete elliptic integral of the second kind

anchor.snippet: The same procedure applies to the complete elliptic integral of the second kind

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the complete elliptic integral E, so this identity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Euler–Mascheroni constant via exponential integral

anchor.section: Double execution for the exponential integral function
anchor.snippet: The Euler–Mascheroni constant

annotations.note: `Real.eulerMascheroniConstant` is defined as `lim (harmonic n - log n)` but the integral representation via Ei used in this example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normal distribution integral

anchor.snippet: The integral of the normal distribution can be evaluated

annotations.note: `integral_gaussian` proves ∫ exp(-b x²) = √(π/b) via the Fubini-style square-and-polar trick (uses `integral_prod_mul`).

annotations.label: Dilogarithm of one via Basel problem

anchor.snippet: This can be applied to the Basel problem

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the Basel sum `hasSum_zeta_two : HasSum (1/n²) (π²/6)` but does not define the dilogarithm or this specific Fubini-derived identity.

annotations.label: Legendre's relation (lemniscatic case)

anchor.snippet: For the lemniscatic special case of Legendre's relation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of Legendre's relation between complete elliptic integrals.
